votes.reason: The Python code accurately translates the Coq definitions, including the string splitting, consonant counting, and filtering logic, producing the exact same results for all concrete inputs.
The Python code accurately translates the Coq definitions, including the vowel check, consonant counting, word splitting, and the pre/spec conditions.
The Python code accurately translates the Coq logic, including the helper functions for counting consonants, splitting words, and the precondition/specification checks.

The Python code faithfully implements the Coq specification. The is_vowel function checks the same characters, count_consonants counts letters that are not vowels using the same ASCII range checks, split_words splits on spaces identically (the Coq version uses rev on accumulated characters which produces the same result as Python's direct append), and select_words filters words with exactly n consonants. The precondition and spec functions also match their Coq counterparts in concrete behavior.

The Python code faithfully implements the same logic as the Coq code: is_vowel checks the same 10 vowel characters, count_consonants counts letters that are not vowels using the same ASCII range checks, split_words splits on spaces identically, and select_words filters words with exactly n consonants. The precondition and spec also match - problem_117_pre checks that all characters are spaces or letters, and problem_117_spec checks equality with select_words output.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code: is_vowel checks the same 10 vowel characters, count_consonants counts letters that are not vowels using the same ASCII range checks (65-90 for uppercase, 97-122 for lowercase), split_words splits on spaces the same way, and select_words filters words with exactly n consonants. The precondition and spec also match - checking that all characters are spaces or letters, and verifying output equals the computed result.

Python mirrors the Coq logic: same vowel set, letter range checks, word splitting on spaces (skipping empties), consonant counting, and filtering. The precondition and spec also match for strings of letters and spaces.
Python mirrors the Coq logic: splits on spaces, counts consonants as ASCII letters excluding vowels, preserves order, and the pre/spec are translated equivalently.

Python mirrors Coq: same vowel set, ASCII letter checks, word splitting on spaces (ignoring multiple/leading/trailing), consonant counting, filtering, and the pre/spec definitions match. Outputs align on all concrete inputs.
